column7: message

Here is a list of some of the things that didn't exist before unions:

    Weekends without work
    All breaks at work, including your lunch breaks
    Paid vacation
    Family & Medical Leave Act (FMLA)
    Sick leave
    Social Security
    Minimum wage
    Civil Rights Act/Title VII - prohibits employer discrimination
    8-hour work day
    Overtime pay
    Child labor laws
    Occupational Safety & Health Act (OSHA)
    40-hour work week
    Workers' compensation (workers' comp)
    Unemployment insurance
    Pensions
    Workplace safety standards and regulations
    Employer health care insurance
    Collective bargaining rights for employees
    Wrongful termination laws
    Age Discrimination in Employment Act of 1967 (ADEA)
    Whistleblower protection laws
    Employee Polygraph Protection Act (EPPA) - prohibits employers from using a lie detector test on an employee
    Veteran's Employment and Training Services (VETS)
    Compensation increases and evaluations (i.e. raises)
    Sexual harassment laws
    Americans With Disabilities Act (ADA)
    Holiday pay
    Employer dental, life, and vision insurance
    Privacy rights
    Pregnancy and parental leave
    Military leave
    The right to strike
    Public education for children
    Equal Pay Acts of 1963 & 2011 - requires employers pay men and women equally for the same amount of work
    Laws ending sweatshops in the United States


We could probably add coal mine wall dusting to that as well, since preventing black lung disease and mine explosions is an objectively good thing.  

I am not really sure where your other claims even come in, like unions being "the worst thieves in history."  How did you come to that conclusions, and what data do you have to support it?

Two questions?

The last work week you worked how many hours did you work a day...on average and were you a child?

@indulgent-marlin Wow, such a long list! But let’s be real, who actually uses all these benefits anyway? And @solid-level, I bet most people here work way more than a “normal” day just to make ends meet. Unions sound great on paper but in reality? Meh.

column7:
Sure. But that's not really the point of your question, if I understand correctly. You're asking the community to respond to whether or not JP understands your atheist position on the matter (I'm also an atheist, fwiw). 

In my experience listening to his work, he is very, very careful not to specifically attribute transcendent meaning to a god-like being. He speaks about this relationship between reality and an ideal in terms of human psychology. Correct me if I'm wrong, but it seems like your understanding of JP is predicated on him equating the transcendent with a god. I'm saying he doesn't do this.
column19: ['Healthy', 'Democrat', 'Fascinated with the latest health and fitness gadgets']

column7:
I've seen some of his work, and I have a suspicion that this clip you saw was taken out of context. He does talk a great deal about the need for the transcendent, but he doesn't really equate this with god or religion.

His evolutionary argument goes something like this: human beings are primates that have brain chemistry that rewards goal-oriented action, over goal attainment. We are hard-wired to fixate on a future outcome and strive to make it a reality. Because we can't know what that outcome actually looks like, we need to simplify it in our minds, in a somewhat purer, more idealized version. That ideal is the transcendent idea that he generally refers to. I think he sees religion as a cultural tool that transmits images of the ideal through time, so that we always have a series of worthy goals that we can aim for, rather than each person having to come up with them from scratch. Again, these ideals are the transcendent, and morality is essentially the pattern of behavior that allows you to achieve these goals within a social framework.

*Edited for typos

Thanks for clarifying, @spry-ship. It sounds like we're on the same page. I appreciate the detailed explanation, too. It helps to see how he frames the transcendent without tying it directly to religious beliefs.

column7: I agree but l don't think that a million dollars is a great number to go off of.. A million just isn't that much, even two decades ago. Of course, l don't just have it laying around, but all that buys is a nice 3-4 bedroom house in a nice city, a couple modest cars, and a little money left to put into savings. If they did this study with people who have a net worth of 25,50, or 100 million+, l think you would see a lot more of that flashy wealth.

Read the book "[The Millionaire Next Door."](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Millionaire_Next_Door)

Much it details the lifestyle of the majority of millionaires in the U.S.: They are a self starters that own their own company and work at it every day. They don't have flashy jobs but run businesses that are "economy proof" providing essential services. And they don't lead flashy lifestyles, but live in modest houses, drive modest vehicles, and so forth. Basically most people who are actually millionaires you wouldn't know it unless they told you, and they do provide backbones to local communities. 

I'll concede that the book is 18 years old, but I can't imagine the data has changed that much. I suspect that in working in estate planning you are far more likely to encounter wealthy people who have complicated and flashy holdings hence the need for planning, whereas the average millionaire doesn't really need any more than a simple will because their estate won't be much different than the every man's, just with some higher numbers on the accounts. 

Edit: To further support my point watch the ESPN "30 for 30: Broke" which documents the high rate in which multi-millionaire athletes go broke after their playing careers end. They have all the legal types in the world advising them, and yet they can't figure out how to hang on to their money. I'll concede that there are other factors in pay there as well, but if it was as easy as, "Hire a financial adviser and you'll never have issues," that wouldn't be such a prevalent thing.

@zesty-integrator Yeah, a million dollars sounds like a lot until you start thinking about actual costs. It’s surprising how fast it can get eaten up by real estate and other expenses.

@amicable-histogram Thanks for sharing that book recommendation! I've heard of it before but never got around to reading it. The idea that many millionaires live modestly makes sense. It’s good to see that wealth doesn’t always translate to flashy lifestyles.

column7:
> They were likely always assholes, yes. 

So why complain, then? 

>But they didn’t act on their shitty beliefs until someone came along and made it okay to do so, as part of a political movement[...]An openly racist, anti-immigrant candidate came along, made political gains, and validated the worst behavior of every shitty person who shares that worldview.

Again, with the kindergarten mentality. You affirmed that they were likely assholes in the first place, yet still continue to pursue passing the buck onto something (politics) just reconfirming what you already thought of them. A bit bizarre, but ok. You excuse their shitty behaviour however, just so you can justify yourself to make out of politics a punching bag where to unload your anger and frustration.                            

It's juvenile of you. They're not assholes because they were ”enabled” (again, this is strictly passing the buck rhetoric, it's nothing meaningful), they are assholes because that's what they are: assholes. You're making out of politics a scapegoat, not addressing the fact that those assholes were rude jerks in the first place. 

>We lived there for years and only ever had to deal with occasional, minor incidents of racism.

QED.

>Or are you seriously arguing that the moment my neighbors (who we’d never interacted with before this) chose to behave threateningly toward my just happened to be in the minutes and days after their candidate was elected, and that was just a fucking coincidence?

Not a coincidence; nor is your premise accurate from where you think about this. Politics didn't changed your neighbour's behaviour, your neighbour was already how he was. If anything, politics just reminded you about neighbour's behaviour, but not that *suddenly* changed & twisted minds.

If you think politics doesn’t affect your daily life, it’s because you’re either not paying attention or because politics have given you the privilege of not being affected negatively. 

Lived in a mostly-white, conservative neighborhood during the last election. Wife is a non-white immigrant green card holder. When Trump’s victory was announced, a couple of his supporters from down the way wandered all the way down to our house, at the end of the cul-de-sac, and started cheering and lighting off fireworks in my driveway. The next day, we found a big pile of dog shit on our front porch, in the center of the doormat. Starting in February of 2017, her green card went through multiple phases of reviews, interviews, and fingerprinting, and still has not been approved for renewal. We also happened to fly back from overseas on the day the travel ban took effect, and got to witness that mess firsthand. So an election appears to have affected us in terms of policy that dictates what we can and can’t do, and affected others in terms of how they treat us.

@intricate-beaujolais I get where you're coming from, but @indulgent-marlin's experience shows that politics can definitely bring out the worst in people. It's not just about personal character, but also about the environment and validation they receive. We can't ignore the impact of political rhetoric on everyday interactions.

column7: I think circumcision is a decent preventative measure against excessive smegma build-up and infection in young boys. It's only acceptable for a parent to clean a child's penis up to a certain point, and then it's on the child to do so for himself. The problem is it's hard to get children to brush their teeth and wash their hands, let alone make a conscientious effort to pull back their foreskin and give their penis a thorough cleaning.

column7:
[More Circumcision Myths You May Believe: Hygiene and STDs](https://www.psychologytoday.com/blog/moral-landscapes/201109/more-circumcision-myths-you-may-believe-hygiene-and-stds)

>Myth:  Uncircumcised penises get smelly smegma.

>Reality check:  Actually, smegma is produced by the genitals of both women and men during the reproductive years.  Smegma is made of sebum and skin cells and lubricates the foreskin and glans in men, and the clitoral hood and inner labia in women.  It is rinsed off during normal bathing and does not cause cancer or any other health problems.

Hygiene is a taught thing, just like if your child won't wash his teeth you won't knock his teeth out of his mouth, you shouldn't mutilate a perfectly healthy organ just because some children may skip on washing it.

@smoky-canal I understand where you're coming from, but @kind-garage makes some valid points. Teaching hygiene seems like a better approach than a surgical decision at birth. Plus, there are plenty of uncircumcised men who keep themselves clean just fine.

column7: As a Swede, I've grown up in a socialist country. These days there is a lot of capitalism mixed in too, obviously, but in talking to my father and grandmother, about how the country was 50 years ago, 80 years ago, when it was a lot more socialist, they have never ever ever ever talked about how people are lazy. People work hard because of purpose, not money. If your place in society is serving a clear and worthwhile purpose, then you work hard because that's what we've always been taught since generations.

column7:
>But could you answer me this question: proportion wise, are Muslim dominated countries far more right wing socially than Christian ones?

I'd say, that if we plotted countries by poverty, that would be a far better indicator of ultraconservative social values with only a few outliers. And what's more, I think the few outliers, Islamic countries that are more conservative than their GDP would otherwise predict, have fairly recent, fairly easy to track reasons for falling off the charts. For instance, Iran was a MUCH more secular country in the 70s than it is now, and would continue to be if it weren't for US intervention.

Your underlying point seems to be that Islam in itself is an extreme force pushing toward social conservatism. My disagreement is that any especially conservative nature of Islamic countries as distinct from predominantly Christian Western countries is mostly in line with relative poverty and relative secularism rather than a core difference in Islam itself.

> I'd say, that if we plotted countries by poverty, that would be a far better indicator of ultraconservative social values with only a few outliers.

Then why are rich Muslim countries like the gulf states more conservative than poverty stricken countries in Latin America or South America?

> For instance, Iran was a MUCH more secular country in the 70s than it is now, and would continue to be if it weren't for US intervention.


Sure, but they got more socially regressive after becoming more Islamic. Why is that? 

> My disagreement is that any especially conservative nature of Islamic countries as distinct from predominantly Christian Western countries is mostly in line with relative poverty and relative secularism rather than a core difference in Islam itself.

Then why are arab states(which are rich relatively) far more conservative than Christian South American countries that aren't as developed?

column7:
>  Right in the middle of those made-up places where you seem to believe that the law is all that's keeping us from reinstating slavery.

You have no idea what I'm talking about.

> For some reason, though, we decide that when you're on the other side of the counter, you don't have those rights anymore. Whereas you can freely choose from whom you BUY, apparently you can't choose to whom you sell. That is inconsistent to me.

What is inconsistent about this? What rights are being violated?

It's this:

> And that's a problem that the free market WILL solve if you're talking about some widespread issue, in a made up town that doesn't exist in this millennium.

Like if you seriously think there aren't towns in America that would be, at the very least, apathetic towards straight up racism against its minority citizens then you're not really operating with a full understanding of the situation.

I mean if racism isn't enough, how about homophobia? Or religious orientation.

It's ridiculous to "respect" a "misguided decision" just because of some weird adherence to libertarianism. Yes, it absolutely does hurt the black community if sandwich shops that don't serve them start popping up.

I'm going to end this conversation with this, I know it's really great to not belong to any outward minorities that would be discriminated against in this way. But this shit affects a bit more than just pissing people off.

You favor the right of a business to make a business decision over the right of a human fucking being to not feel like a second class citizen.

@uniform-decision, you know what they say, "If you can't take a joke, maybe it's because you're too sensitive." Everyone has the right to run their business how they see fit. If someone doesn't like it, they can go somewhere else. Life's not a bed of roses for everyone, get used to it!
"@independent-base Please ensure your comments contribute positively to the discussion and avoid language that could be seen as dismissive of others' feelings or experiences."

column7:
The company is named hoverboard,  for the purpose of selling a "hoverboard"   

This isn't "Hoffman-Verio Board Articulation Division"  

It's Hoverboard,  a single word.  A bit different from Swatch-Mercedes Art - smart.  Which no basis in the idea of "smartcars"  that we have today.    Swatch Mercedes Art,  was the internal designation used by MCC,  Mercedes City Car and Micro Compact Cars (Swatch's subsidiary) for the eco car they were working on.  The original name was the Swatchcar,   which Mercedes wasn't keen on,  and said they had to use a company neutral name.  So they simply went with the abbreviated name from the internal designation SMArt.   

You can't suggest that something akin occurred with hoverboard technologies.   Then again,  their name is also Hoverboard,  not "SMART"  which happens to sell automobiles, none of which are actually called "smart cars"   except by the public.   Technically they're smart (make)  and the model,  such as smart prime,  smart passion, smart pure,  and smart proxy.    

With hoverboard,   no matter the name,  it is Hoverboard (make)  model... meaning the term that has expectation not met by the company,  right there.  It's misleading, smart is not.

column7: Doesn't the fact that they appropriated the term rather than coining it themselves reinforce OP's view of their incompetence?

From what I can tell - Social Justice Warrior is a term that comes from the social justice movement itself, and is not a name what was created by the "anti-social justice movement". 

http://english.stackexchange.com/questions/202080/origin-of-the-phrase-social-justice-warrior

Therefore,the "anti-social-justice" crowd is just using a term that was already pre-existing. Its not inherently an insult, its just become due to the way that it has been used. (Like moron and imbecile were technical terms once upon a time before they were used colloquially).
@quick-cheese Thanks for the info! Seems like context and intent really matter here. The term's evolution shows how language changes over time.

Partisanship does not exist because parties exist. Parties exist because partisanship exists. Just look back at the reasons why the first parties were created: like-minded people wanted the best shot to implement their policies.

column7: FDR also abused the hell out of the Constitution, and expanded the Governments Powers to unsustainable levels, and I'd take anything he said with a grain of salt.

Minimum wage law was passed with the idea in mind that it would provide a decent standard of living. FDR said that any company that relies on paying its workers less than a living wage did not deserve to operate in the US. So why do businesses deserve to take advantage of individuals by paying them a substandard living wage?

@rectilinear-tree The issue isn't about taking advantage; it's about the reality of business costs and market dynamics. Not every business can afford a high minimum wage without cutting jobs or raising prices. Let's focus on solutions that don't harm small businesses.

column7:
I don't accept it as a *real* field of study, I think it's a BS university course for one thing. Might as well do David Beckham studies (that's a 'real' course too apparently). 

I'm not opressing anyone, I just think it's wrong. 

Question. Are you asking other people to call you queer, are you ok with that? 

Are women queers now, when did that happen.

> I don't accept it as a real field of study, I think it's a BS university course

When world-renowned universities have whole queer studies departments and multitudes of course offerings within those departments, what makes it a "fake" field of study?

I didn't say you're oppressing anyone, I asked you why you want to police *my* use of the term. Your view is that I shouldn't use it, I'm only using it for "shock value," and that my use of the term should be "censored." I have zero interest in debating whether, if implemented, your view would constitute oppression. I am quite interested in why you think you know my oppression better than me. Why should this word offend me? If it doesn't offend me, then why do you think it should offend you?

column7: @brass-column It’s important to understand that Queer Studies is about understanding and analyzing the experiences, histories, and cultures of LGBTQ+ people. Dismissing it as a "BS" course shows a lack of awareness about its significance. And yes, the term "queer" has been reclaimed by many in the community as a way to embrace their identities. It’s not about shocking anyone but about self-identification and empowerment.
